data:
SC Decision Topology Overview Roles Network Map Meeting Architecture KPI Matrix Authority Map Cross-Functional Seams Priorities

Supply Chain Decision-Making Topology

Diagnostic Brief — US Automotive OEM (Anonymized) · Toyota-Scale Manufacturing
5 Interviewees 6 Sub-Functions 10 Recurring Forums ~350 SC Employees ~$2B Direct Materials Spend Executive · Director · Manager Tiers
The supply chain function operates with a coherent tiered governance architecture and well-understood financial delegation thresholds. However, material gaps exist in three areas: (1) the monthly S&OP working session — the intended load-bearing cross-functional alignment mechanism — underperforms under pressure with no designated chair; (2) cross-functional decisions requiring Engineering commitment have no formal mechanism below VP level, creating multi-month unresolved supplier issues; (3) decision documentation from verbal alignment to written execution direction is inconsistently practiced across tiers, creating cascade failures.
5
Forums functioning as true decision venues
3
Forums with governance gaps or ambiguous ownership
13
KPIs identified across 5 interviewees
9
KPIs with shared / contested ownership
5
Cross-functional seams with partial or ambiguous clarity
4mo
Longest unresolved supplier disposition (casting — Engineering backlog)

1Interviewee Role Profiles

Five roles interviewed spanning Executive to Manager seniority across four sub-functions.

Patricia Chen
VP, Supply Chain
Executive Overall SC Leadership
Reports to: COO (Marcus Webb)
Direct reports: 6 Directors (Procurement, Supplier Quality, Logistics, Production Planning, Inventory, Strategy & Analytics)
Scope: End-to-end SC accountability from upstream sourcing through plant-floor delivery. Chairs Supply Chain Leadership Meeting & Exec S&OP.
David Okonkwo
Director, Direct Materials Procurement
Director Procurement
Reports to: Patricia Chen (VP)
Direct reports: 8 buyers, 4 analysts
Scope: ~$2B production materials spend across ~200 suppliers. Accountable for landed cost, supplier OTD, and first-pass quality rate.
Sarah Martinez
Director, Production Planning & Scheduling
Director Production Planning
Reports to: Patricia Chen (VP)
Direct reports: 6 planners (master scheduling, materials, demand)
Scope: Master production schedule, MRP process, S&OP facilitation. Accountable for schedule attainment, shortage incidents, inventory days on hand.
Marcus Thompson
Senior Manager, Commodity Purchasing (Metals)
Senior Manager Procurement
Reports to: David Okonkwo (Director)
Scope: ~$600M annual metals spend (steel, aluminum) across 35 suppliers. Accountable for unit cost, supplier OTD, and scrap/waste minimization.
James Patel
Plant Supply Chain Manager (Lead Assembly)
Manager Plant-Level / Cross-Functional
Reports to: Director of Operations (plant); dotted line to central SC
Direct reports: 8 (inbound receipt, inventory control, local coordination, shortage escalation)
Scope: Plant-floor materials execution — on-time staging, inventory accuracy, shortage escalation. Interface between central SC decisions and local manufacturing reality.
Coverage note: This cohort covers 4 of 6 sub-functions (Leadership, Procurement, Production Planning, Plant-level). Supplier Quality, Logistics, and Strategy & Analytics directors are not yet interviewed. The semiconductor/electronics commodity manager — identified as strategically elevated post-COVID — is a notable gap for the next interview wave.

2Decision-Making Network Map

Roles (nodes), forums (nodes), and connections showing who attends what and where authority flows. Color-coded by sub-function, layered by seniority.

VP / Executive
Director (Procurement)
Director (Planning)
Senior Manager
Manager / Plant
Quarterly Forum
Monthly Forum (functional)
⚠️ Governance Gap
High-cadence operational
Key pattern: Patricia Chen is the hub of the network — connecting upward to COO, downward to all Directors, and present in all four strategic/monthly forums. The Monthly S&OP (⚠️) sits at the critical junction between strategic direction and operational execution but has the most ambiguous governance. James Patel's dotted-line reporting creates a structural tension between plant-level accountability and central supply chain authority.

3Meeting Architecture

All recurring supply chain forums ordered from strategic (quarterly) to operational (daily). ⚠️ indicates documented governance gaps.

QUARTERLY

Executive S&OP

✅ True Decision Forum

Reviews and sanctions strategic planning framework — capacity expansion, major supplier contracts, network changes, capital decisions. Framework-level governance for horizon-3 supply chain decisions.

Attendees: Patricia Chen, COO, CFO, Sales leadership, Manufacturing VP, Sarah Martinez (SME resource)

Outputs: Sanctioned plans and capital approvals cascaded to monthly S&OP

Quarterly Plant SC Review

⚠️ Governance Gap

Surfaces plant-level SC performance issues, aligns on process improvements. Structurally appropriate as plant-central bridge but operates as status report rather than accountability review.

Attendees: James Patel, Plant Manager, Dir. of Operations, Patricia Chen, Relevant Directors (ad hoc)

⚠️ No named action-tracking owner between quarters. $15K premium freight threshold review has general agreement but no assigned owner with dates.

MONTHLY

Monthly S&OP (Working Session)

⚠️ Underperforming

Intended to finalize demand plan, resolve demand-supply gaps, authorize tactical inventory and capacity decisions. Underperforms under pressure — alignment migrates to bilateral VP calls.

Attendees: Patricia Chen, Sales VP, Manufacturing VP, Finance, Dir. Strategy & Analytics (agenda-setter), Sarah Martinez (facilitator)

⚠️ No designated chair with accountability for forum effectiveness. Attendance degrades under operational pressure. Decision-authority substitutes attend.

SC Leadership Meeting

✅ True Decision Forum

Reviews sub-function KPI performance, closes structural decisions above Director authority, surfaces and resolves cross-sub-function issues. Patricia chairs; Directors are accountable nodes.

Attendees: Patricia Chen (chair), all 6 Directors, Finance, Operations (unconfirmed)

⚠️ Decision documentation inconsistent — verbal alignment doesn't consistently cascade as written direction to execution teams.

Monthly Supplier Perf. Review

✅ Decision Forum

Reviews supplier scorecards, resolves root cause attribution disputes, authorizes corrective action plans and inspection level changes. Closes real decisions within Procurement + Quality authority.

Attendees: David Okonkwo (chair), Supplier Quality team, Production Planning, Buyers/analysts

⚠️ Engineering does NOT attend — disposition decisions requiring engineering sign-off cannot close here. Items referred informally with no SLA.

BI-WEEKLY

Bi-Weekly Materials Planning Sync

✅ Most Operational (Mon + Thu)

Near-term supply coverage review, shortage identification, expedite prioritization, root cause attribution for delivery misses. Highest-cadence cross-sub-function forum. Most action-oriented below VP level.

Attendees: David Okonkwo / senior buyer, Sarah Martinez's planning team, James Patel (plant)

⚠️ Premium freight above $15K must route to Commodity Mgr → Procurement Director — approval latency documented as causing near-stoppage events.

WEEKLY

Weekly Operations Review

✅ True Decision Forum

Near-term operational issue resolution across SC, Manufacturing, Finance, and Engineering. Patricia describes this as the most action-oriented meeting she attends. Cross-functional, chaired by COO.

Attendees: Patricia Chen, Manufacturing VP, Finance, Engineering, COO (chair)

Weekly Procurement Team Mtg

✅ Decision Forum

In-function decision-making on order placement, escalations, supplier actions within David's authority. Standard lean sub-function review — decisions close in the room for items within Director authority.

Attendees: David Okonkwo, 8 buyers, 4 analysts

Weekly 1:1s (Patricia ↕ Reports; Patricia ↑ COO)

ℹ️ Informational / Soft Escalation

Pulse checks, proactive loop-ins on issues approaching escalation thresholds. Functionally substitutes for formal forums when those forums are too slow. No formal decisions close here.

Attendees: Patricia + each of 6 Directors (individual); Patricia + COO

⚠️ Variability in structure across 6 director 1:1s — self-identified by Patricia as inconsistency.

DAILY

Daily Production Stand-Up (7am)

⚠️ Mixed — Decision + Info

Prior-day schedule attainment review, same-day recovery decisions, shortage and risk flagging. Closest to lean tier-1 daily management meeting — structured, time-boxed, floor-level. Perception gap: James Patel calls it info-sharing; Sarah Martinez calls it a decision forum.

Attendees: Sarah Martinez, Manufacturing supervision, James Patel; Manufacturing VP (when significant issues)

Architecture assessment: The meeting cadence is structurally sound — from quarterly strategic framework through daily operational execution — and consistent with TPS-influenced tiered management. The critical failure point is the Monthly S&OP working session, which sits at the most important integration node (demand-supply alignment) but has the weakest governance design. The daily stand-up and bi-weekly materials sync are the two most operationally effective forums in the architecture.

4KPI Ownership Matrix

All identified KPIs mapped to owners, measurement frequency, review forum, and ownership type. 13 KPIs identified — 9 with shared or contested accountability.

KPI Ownership Distribution
KPI Sub-Function Primary Owner Ownership Type Review Frequency Key Forum
Overall Supply Chain Leadership — Patricia Chen (VP)
Landed Cost (Total) Leadership Patricia Chen; Finance (measurement) ⚠️ Shared Monthly SC Leadership Mtg; Exec S&OP
Supplier Quality Index Leadership Patricia Chen (accountable); Dir. Supplier Quality (operational) ✅ Sole (SC side) Monthly SC Leadership Meeting
On-Time Delivery to Plants (OTD) Leadership Patricia Chen; Manufacturing VP ⚠️ Shared Monthly / Quarterly SC Leadership Mtg; Exec S&OP
Inventory Turns Leadership Patricia Chen; Sales (demand signal); Manufacturing (schedule stability) ⚠️ Shared Monthly / Quarterly SC Leadership Mtg; Exec S&OP
Procurement — David Okonkwo (Director) & Marcus Thompson (Sr. Manager)
Landed Cost per Unit Procurement David Okonkwo ✅ Sole Monthly SC Leadership Mtg; Supplier Perf. Review
Cost Avoidance Procurement David Okonkwo ✅ Sole Monthly SC Leadership Meeting
Supplier On-Time Delivery (OTD) Procurement David Okonkwo (supplier side); Sarah Martinez (forecast side) ⚠️ Shared / Contested Bi-weekly / Monthly Materials Planning Sync; Supplier Perf. Review
First-Pass Quality Rate Procurement David Okonkwo (supplier selection); Supplier Quality Dir. (measurement) ⚠️ Shared Monthly Monthly Supplier Perf. Review
Unit Cost (Metals) Procurement Marcus Thompson ✅ Sole Bi-weekly Bi-weekly Supplier Perf. Review
Scrap & Waste Minimization (Metals) Procurement Marcus Thompson (spec compliance); Quality Engineer (measurement) ⚠️ Shared Bi-weekly Bi-weekly Supplier Perf. Review
Production Planning — Sarah Martinez (Director)
Schedule Attainment Planning Sarah Martinez (nominally sole); Manufacturing (equipment/labor); Procurement (delivery) ⚠️ Shared in practice Daily / Monthly Daily Stand-Up; Monthly S&OP
Shortage Incidents Planning Sarah Martinez (aggregate); James Patel (plant); Central SC 🔴 No single owner (aggregate) Daily / Bi-weekly / Monthly Stand-Up; Materials Sync; S&OP
Inventory Days on Hand Planning Sarah Martinez (primary); Sales (demand signal); Manufacturing (schedule stability) ⚠️ Shared Monthly Monthly S&OP; SC Leadership Mtg
Plant-Level — James Patel (Manager)
On-Time Material Delivery to Production Plant-Level James Patel (execution); Sarah Martinez (plan changes) ⚠️ Shared Daily / Bi-weekly Daily Stand-Up; Materials Sync
Inventory Accuracy (Plant) Plant-Level James Patel ✅ Sole Ongoing cycle counts Weekly Plant Operations Meeting
⚠️ OTD appears across three sub-functions under different labels — "On-Time Delivery to Plants" (Patricia), "Supplier On-Time Delivery" (David), and "On-Time Material Delivery to Production" (James). These are related but genuinely distinct measurement points with different owners. Root cause attribution when any OTD KPI misses is a recurring contested issue between Planning and Procurement, with no formal RACI documented.
KPI ownership pattern: Of 14 KPIs identified, only 4 have unambiguously sole ownership (Landed Cost/Unit, Cost Avoidance, Unit Cost Metals, Inventory Accuracy Plant). The remaining 10 are shared with contested or distributed accountability. Shortage Incidents is the most structurally concerning — tracked at three levels with no single named owner for aggregate trend management.

5Decision Authority Map

Decisions organized by tier showing what types close at each level, in which forums, and where authority is clear vs. ambiguous.

Decision Clarity by Tier
Where Decisions Actually Close

Executive — VP, Supply Chain (Patricia Chen)

Financial delegation: up to $50M. Cross-functional alignment: formal + bilateral. Escalation to COO: judgment-based heuristic.
✓
Supplier sourcing contracts up to $50M annually
Forum: SC Leadership Meeting; bilateral with COO for complex cases
✓
Supplier disqualification / controlled shipping authorization (unilateral when speed required)
Forum: SC Leadership Meeting or unilateral
✓
Demand plan finalization — closes contested decisions in Monthly S&OP
Forum: Monthly S&OP
!
Inventory corrective action (cross-functional)
Forum: Bilateral calls with Sales + Manufacturing VPs; Exec S&OP for major shifts
⚠️ Patricia cannot drive unilaterally — formal path (Exec S&OP) is too slow; actual path is bilateral ad hoc
✓
Logistics network decisions (carrier contracts, 3PL, routing)
Forum: SC Leadership Meeting; within budget authority
✓
Dual-sourcing strategy authorization
Forum: SC Leadership Meeting; Weekly Ops Review for urgent cases

Above-VP — COO (Marcus Webb) / Executive Committee

Financial threshold: >$50M. Trigger: major capital, network restructuring, multi-year strategic commitments.
✓
Supplier contracts above $50M annually (CFO visibility required)
Forum: Executive S&OP; direct COO engagement
⚠️ Decision documentation and cascade back to VP execution team noted as inconsistent
✓
Major capital implications, network restructuring
Forum: Executive S&OP
!
Multi-year strategic supplier commitments (especially where quality issues co-exist)
Forum: Weekly Ops Review and/or direct escalation to COO
⚠️ Threshold is judgment-based ("would Marcus want to know") — not codified or shared with Directors

Director — David Okonkwo & Sarah Martinez

Financial delegation: contracts below $50M within existing relationships. Cross-functional: limited — depends on bilateral relationships. Schedule authority: within 2-week rolling window.
✓
Sourcing contracts & order placement within existing supplier relationships (below $50M)
Forum: Weekly Procurement Team Meeting; bilateral with VP for strategic implications
✓
New supplier qualification / dual-sourcing initiation (authorization, not execution)
Forum: SC Leadership Meeting (authorization); Supplier Perf. Review (evidence base)
!
Corrective action plan authorization — contested root cause cases (supplier vs. Planning forecast)
Forum: Monthly Supplier Perf. Review
⚠️ Cannot close without VP escalation or bilateral resolution when root cause is disputed
✓
Master schedule sequence changes within 2-week rolling window
Forum: Daily Production Stand-Up (Sarah's unilateral authority)
!
Demand plan changes with commercial implication
Forum: Monthly S&OP — requires Sales alignment
⚠️ Sarah cannot close unilaterally — dependent on Sales VP presence and engagement in S&OP
!
Premium freight authorization above $15K (plant-originated)
Forum: Escalated James Patel → Commodity Manager → David Okonkwo
⚠️ Path is clear but approval latency documented — 36-hour timeline caused near-stoppage incident
✕
Engineering disposition requests (tolerance relaxations, spec changes)
Forum: Ad hoc — no standing forum, no SLA
🔴 CRITICAL GAP: 4-month unresolved casting disposition (~$200–300K annual cost). Engineering queue has no SLA for SC requests. Only escalation trigger = line-down event.
